speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 12. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. You were telling me about the Korean War.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was informed informally that I should prepare myself to be called up. In about two months, they'd need to-- to active duty. I was in the reserve because they could use my capacities. And I would be assigned to-- the idea was to assign me to General Eisenhower's headquarters in Paris. It was actually in-- in Versailles. And I didn't want to go back to Europe yet. Rafael, my oldest son, had just been born. And Lore was new in America. I wanted to stay here. Well, in order to for-- to avoid this, I went to the [INAUDIBLE] office and told him I am very much interested in penology. And I am a psychologist, too. And I would like to serve in this capacity in the Army. He said, we can use you very much. We have problems with-- with desertions and just-- just with all kinds of crimes committed by Army people. And so they took me and-- and be to it the regular call-up. Before I was-- when I was called up, I was already in the [? north-- ?] in-- in the [? one of ?] [INAUDIBLE]. [? I was ?] go to [INAUDIBLE]. It was under the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is-- inspector general, I think. Adjutant general, under the adjutant general's office was the penal system. And I became chief psychologist for-- for [INAUDIBLE], where I introduced groups to [INAUDIBLE], unknown then, and persons [? for ?] completely unknown. And it was a [? highly ?] known activity in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your final rank when you left the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your final rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lieutenant Colonel. I was retired as a Lieutenant Colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in College Park at that time. I wasn't-- not in active duty. I went once every-- once a month, I went to these meetings. And once a year, I went to a two-weeks training mission, which consisted in my being, [? you ?] [? see, ?] working as a psychologist at the Walter Reed Army Hospital, just consulted in psychotherapy, or at the Naval Hospital in Bethesda for two weeks once a year. And when I became 60 years old, which was in 1966, I was transferred to the-- to the retired reserve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about some of the other things you did in this country since the war besides your work with the military? How did you make your living in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- first of all, I started-- I went to Columbia University to get an American degree because I was-- knew a lot about psychology. But psychology was entirely different here than in Europe. The Europeans psychologists saw the American psychologist as somebody who takes a rat and weighs the feces of the rat. And then four weeks later, he weighs it again. [? It's ?] [? a ?] [? difference ?] [? in ?] [? psychology, ?] [? I said. ?] But the American psychology saw the European psychologist as someone who's sitting back on a chair while-- while a patient tells his story, and he reads a book. So I had to Americanize my outlook. And I went to Columbia, and I attended a lot of classes at Columbia very intensively for about one year. And after the year, I found myself on a bulletin board because I qualified for the [INAUDIBLE], which I took in this year for their master's examination, comprehensive master's examination. So I took it and made a [INAUDIBLE] and got a master's degree from Columbia in psychology. And then I was in private practice for a short time. I-- we lived in Maryland. And I did some counseling there. But I was the still quite unsure of my capacities in psychology. And when the Korean War came, I became chief psychologist in Leavenworth. After four years there, I was transferred to Germany. And I was in charge of psychology for 1,000 air commanders. [? Was ?] [? there ?] [? a ?] [? whole ?] [INAUDIBLE] of Americans there. And there, I made my living by being in the Army. The Army pays quite well. And then I got out and became chief psychologist for the District of Columbia for the welfare system. They had-- the District of Columbia had-- their-- all juvenile delinquents were committed to the District of Columbia. They were committed to the Children's Center. And-- and I was in charge of this. And after 10 years, I was [INAUDIBLE] area. I left the civil service and became consultant for a year to the-- in delinquency for the government of the state of Nebraska, which was that I worked during the week there. And on the weekends, I always flew back. It was-- I could fly as a retired-- as a military reservist, I would fly militarily. And there was a plane going every day, every week. On Friday, it left from-- the Air Force base. There was a big Air Force base there. And it was-- it was not in Nebraska. It was in Kansas, to-- to-- to-- Offutt-- Offutt Air Force Base, through here to Andrews. And then Sunday evening, I flew back to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spent every weekend here. And after a year, I-- my report was completed there. And I went into private practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- now in between, I had been sent in Germany, from '55 to '58. And there, too, complete therapeutic training in both the Freudian method-- in all three, the Freudian method, the Adlerian method, and the Jungian method, which was unusual. They don't go together, but I had them altogether and got a certificate, which-- which enabled me to practice psychotherapy in Germany, which I, of course, never did. But I for now was secure. And I had some private practice, mainly in marriage counseling. I did very much marriage counseling. I did very little depth therapy. I've [INAUDIBLE] [? to do ?] this. I had seen so much of life. And I, myself, [INAUDIBLE] a very happy marriage, [? so ?] I did mainly marriage counseling.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was also teaching at George Washington University. I started as an associate lecturer and was-- within a year, was full-time professorial status of the-- of the chief of the [? unit. ?] And I was consultant [? over ?] to- to Walter Reed Army Hospital and consultant to the counseling center of the University of Maryland there on a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You've also written--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You've also written several books.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your writings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wrote which-- which I had on my mind all the time to write and which had-- which had two more editions under the name of-- of, actually, first a hardcover edition and then a softcover edition of-- of the same subject as the-- the same books. There's only very little changes inside. And had an additional chapter, which deals mainly with Lore and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I wrote Did you read it? I was-- this was very much on my mind, too. And I wrote many articles, psychological professional articles-- I think 12 or 14-- which were transferred to other languages. What kind of research went into your writing? I've-- I have your books. And I've noted--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you've-- you've got original source material.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you find some of these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well,--

speaker: interviewer
text: --original documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my older son, he's employed as a research psychologist by the Library of Congress. And he's-- he provided me with many data. And I had an-- I had a friend-- he had a friend, Richard. I called him Rick-- I don't know now his second name; doesn't come to my mind now-- who was very interested in Holocaust and was a real researcher, who helped me very much by getting me data from the Library of Congress. And I always had people who were more as friends of mine and who went for me to the various libraries and looked up what I wanted to know. And I was, for-- for this year, for the [INAUDIBLE], I was in England for about three months and studied there very carefully in the Public Records Office, which is their national archive. What they had there is I have many documentage people-- [? other ?] [? people ?] [? that ?] [? was ?] [? interested ?] [? in it ?] and which I discovered is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned one son. Did-- did you have more than one child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one is a lawyer and with the Library of Congress. He's one of the better-informed men about notorious men's [? drugs ?] now, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rafael Perl. He's on many panels, and he wrote some books-- I think two books. Well, he wrote another book, too, on [INAUDIBLE] the Falkland Islands. It's a legal situation of the Falkland Islands, when the war-- the dispute was between Great Britain and Argentina. And-- and the younger son is a veterinarian, who has a-- he doesn't want to work for anybody and want to be-- doesn't want to be tied to any-- [? tied down. ?] So he-- very original. He established himself as a house-call veterinarian. And [? there's ?] a tremendous demand for it. He makes house calls. And he-- he-- many people prefer not to go to the veterinarian's office. They don't have the time. They are busy during the daytime. They are afraid that their cat or the dog might get into fights there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- he charges for coming there what a plumber would charge, he says, for coming. And then he charges a little less than the others-- than you have to pay to others when you come to their office. And he keeps up very cleverly the contact there. He calls the next morning and says, this is Dr. Perl. I waned to enquire how Fifi is doing. Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, four. The older son, Rafael, has two, Gili, 6, and Ori, 4 years old. They're both-- their native tongue is both of them is Hebrew, although they were born here. But they're-- they're bilingual. They go twice a year to Israel. His wife is [INAUDIBLE] in [? Israeli. ?] And she was correspondent of the [INAUDIBLE], which was one of the biggest Israeli newspapers for Washington until a year ago, when they were [? bought ?] [? folding ?] up. Now she's correspondent for the Tel Aviv radio, military radio. She's, everyday at least once on the radio there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Well, ?] the Solomon has two children, one, Jonathon Elliott, who is the happiest child I ever saw. And the daughter [INAUDIBLE], who is now about 2 months old, Juliana Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a few more questions I wanted to ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you had spoken several times, in person and on the telephone, with Eichmann. I want to know your contact with him during the trial. I know you-- you mentioned to me that you were at the Eichmann trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what role you played and if you had any contact with Eichmann at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't speak to him several time. I spoke to him once when I-- when he interrogated me and put me through the [? wall ?] and said he would make me a second belly button or a second [? asshole. ?] And then when he came and said, [NON-ENGLISH], [INAUDIBLE] transport [? you ?] [? out. ?] And then I spoke to him over the-- there were two [? personal ?] [? contacts. ?] There was only two personal, very short contacts. Then I had two telephone contacts, when I called him from-- spoke to him. I don't remember how I came to speak to him. But I called him from-- from Arnoldstein when his train was stuck there and threatened him with [? the ?] [? masada ?] [? repetition. ?] And then when I spoke to him from Romania, which ended with, we'll still-- we'll-- we'll still get you, Perl. [? Well, I ?] had two personal-- very short personal contacts and two very short telephone contacts. And I was called to the Eichmann trial at the invitation of Hausner, who was the-- who was the chief-- chief prosecutor. And-- and he wanted to interview me. He wanted to have me ready for the case that Eichmann should say that he cooperated in the rescue of so many Jews because all this wouldn't have done without-- been done without his help. But I knew that he had to be forced by-- by-- by higher up. So when I was there, waiting to be called possibly, well, while-- while still the defense was still on, his lawyer, Servatius, had heard that I am here and I'm alive. Was not a usual thing for a Jew who [INAUDIBLE] around in Europe at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- he intended to bring this up as a way to show that Eichmann actually acted on orders, but where he could, he helped, though he-- it would have backfired. So Hausner said to him, [? we will ?] [? not call ?] and we will not speak about it if you'll keep away from this. [? You then ?] go home. And in case he-- he comes to [? this, ?] I will call you back. Be ready to be called.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wasn't called back. I was for about three weeks waiting in Jerusalem to be called. And I had no contact with Eichmann at that time whatsoever. I was sitting in the audience, and I saw him sitting there with very-- with a feeling of very good satisfaction, [? the ?] [? nemesis. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You are credited with saving 40,000 Jewish lives. Earlier in the interview, you said that you participated in saving perhaps 17,000 or 18,000 lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain the discrepancy? Why do people credit you with saving 40,000?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had started the whole business. Nobody dared to do it. I started it with the other soldiers [INAUDIBLE]. And the others jumped on the bandwagon. And they had much more money at their disposal, the regular Zionist organization, than we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they brought more people in even than we had brought in. But the whole thing is run under the name of Perl Transporter. And there was even one crook, who sold a trip to Palestine illegal in Romania and called the ship And we were all horrified what had happened to the people. And do you [INAUDIBLE] know what happened? He [INAUDIBLE] as possible. He became to the coast and was caught by them. And they brought [? him. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the ship was lost, of course and the people there sent to the camp. And after two or three months, they were free. So the [? Los ?] [? Perlos ?] was a success.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but I'm-- I'm [? fully ?] [? credited ?] with 40,000. I'm-- maybe if you say that-- that I opened the ways for it, and that I showed the way, then there would be 40,000. And why just I? Because I started it, and then I'm a somewhat [? colorful ?] person. So it was easy to concentrate on me. You did not mention what happened to your brother. And you did not mention your parents. Were your parents alive at--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: --around the time of the Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both had dies before Anschluss. My father died in Prague in 1935. And my mother died in January 1938 in Vienna. And my brother was on the first-- [? last ?] transport from Vienna, which was the first [INAUDIBLE] was 358. [INAUDIBLE] to the people assembled there. He was on this transport. And he was in-- in the movie business. He imported films-- imported films to Israel and was very well known in Israel. He has a very, very engaging personality. Was a very good-- good-looking man. He was four years younger than I. And I had just, a few days ago, the-- the documents, which his wife collected when he was-- when he died of a heart attack in 1973. He died of a heart attack. He got letters from all over the world, [INAUDIBLE] feelings of-- of sympathy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have been honored by many people in many countries. Can you tell me some of the acknowledgements and recognitions you've received for your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, some thing which I told you very much is the letter which I got from President Reagan, which is a very powerful letter, very beautifully worded. I was the Man of the Year [INAUDIBLE] from the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles. I got two or three other awards from them. I got an award from the state of California on my-- it was on my 80th birthday. It was not an 80th birthday. [? Just ?] [? how-- ?] somehow differently. The capital flew a little flag [? for to-- ?] all day long in my honor. And the flag was then given to me [? to have ?] it. I have a certificate from the-- I think it was a architect of the-- of the capital [INAUDIBLE]. I have been honored by the Jewish-- Jewish community of Philadelphia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got a [? Jabotinsky ?] Order. I'm looking at them, what I have there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now as long as you mentioned [? Jabotinsky, ?] can you-- can you tell me a little bit about him? You-- you knew him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him well, yes. I knew him-- I knew him before I started to organize the illegal transport-- the free transports. I knew him before that because I was actually when I was one of the leaders of the Austrian Revisionist Movement, and he was the leader for the whole world. And I knew him from the congresses. And when I worked in Romania, his-- his sister's had-- his sister's apartment-- her name was Epstein; she was married to a Romanian Jewish surgeon-- was our headquarters. And that's where we [? were ?] [? back ?] from, from Mrs. Epstein's headquarters. And I-- he came to visit her once, twice. Once was a more personal visit. And once he-- he me with the king there, though I had more contact with him than there. And I had contact with him when I went from Vienna through to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: I noticed in one of your books you've got a few words that were written by Prime Minister Begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about? How did he come to write--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Begin-- I knew Begin very fleetingly only. He was a young [? Betarine ?] leader, and I was a [? man ?] in the [INAUDIBLE] movement. And I knew him from there, from the meeting in Krakow. And then, he came once with a troop of-- with a group of [? Betarines ?] and others. And they had organized an illegal transport to Palestine. And they were stuck on the border of Romania and-- and Yugoslavia. The Romanians didn't let them in. And I went in there and spoke to him. And we couldn't get them through. This was one of our failures. And he went back to Warsaw, and most of the people were killed. And when I wrote the book, I sent it to him. And so [? who-- ?] [INAUDIBLE] gave him the book, Ulbricht, with whom I worked [INAUDIBLE] for the transport. He was his-- his-- I don't know what his title was. But he was his right-hand man, his left-hand man. His right-hand man was another person. And he said about me something of which I'm very proud. That's how he-- he wrote the foreword for And it ends, your deeds, Dr. Perl, will be record-- will be remembered forever by all Jews, in fact, by all men of [? goodwill. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I do have just a few more questions. I want to tie up some loose ends. I had asked you very early on in the interview if you had used any false names. And I know we talked about one false name that you were-- that you had used--

speaker: interviewee
text: To have.

speaker: interviewer
text: --during your-- when you were doing the transports. But you also used a false name when you were interrogating prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used the name Clinton.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used the name Clinton.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just thought I wanted to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Clinton is what-- I didn't know of the present president. But there was a hotel, Governor Clinton, at the Pennsylvania Station. And I knew this hotel. And when I was asked of any preference, I said, yes, make it Clinton.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was something else that you said very early on that I wanted to get clarification on. You said that you might have testified on behalf of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of [INAUDIBLE] [? Jakowitsch. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If the trial had been in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you would not do it in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wanted you to explain that. I didn't understand what you were talking about. So could you clarify what you meant by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I wouldn't testify before an Austrian court, where the judges are Nazi, and the-- and the members of the jury are Nazis, and [? they all ?] [? are-- ?] [? they're very ?] [? good ?] Nazis. And in Israel, it would have been different. But I might not have probably give-- testified in Israel either because of his-- of his involvement with the-- with the deportation of Jews from Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your mind, do you think that justice was served by the War Crimes trials, not just the ones that you were involved in but also the Nuremberg trials? In your opinion, did you think justice was served?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who were accused were found guilty to hang. But most of them were not hanged. And then very-- I mean, huge number of those who were involved directly in the killing were not accused. And on the ally's side, people who-- who should have stood, too, [? there, ?] [? too, ?] Mr.-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what's his name? In the state department. I can't recall the name right now. And the whole state department organized-- organized-- they were responsible, together with the Germans, who were responsible. The Germans put the house afire, and the state department organized the blockade of Europe. The conference in-- in Evian, which was hold supposedly in order to help the-- the poor people-- this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, We have to pause now to change tape.
